creator: chris@chrullrich.net
text:
(In reply to Palle Girgensohn from comment #5)

> Most postgresql folk tend to say "don't do it", since it doesn't scale
> very well. That is because it requires manual mapping of the roles
> between Kerberos and the PostgreSQL roles. 

That is not correct. In an environment designed for SSO, which is what Kerberos is superbly good at after all, you just name the database roles the same as the Kerberos users. For simple transformations, like dropping the realm name, there is pg_ident.conf.

> That said, the support exists, and it should just work.

It barely exists, and it does not work from PostgreSQL 16 on.

> The biggest complication is that there is a heimdal in the base
> system, and another number of librarires, two heimdal and four
> MIT-krb5, that can be picked as dependencies. It seems easier now

USE=gssapi, as illustrated in the two proposed patches (one here, the other is mine in #220325), avoids this problem.

Also, there is:

- one ancient Heimdal in base that you should not concern yourself with,
- one current Heimdal in ports, security/heimdal, that CONFLICTS with
  the MIT ports
- two versions of MIT Kerberos in ports, security/krb5-120 and -121,
  that have CONFLICTS with each other and with Heimdal
- a wrapper port, security/krb5, that integrates exactly one of the
  versioned ports

By my count, that is a total of one port that can exist in a system at any given time.

> that the Heimdal port installs in their own directory, lib/heimdal.
> It was at some point suggested that a FreeBSD base system built
> without kerberos was necessary to accurately verify the port.
> I dunno if that has changed?

That suggestion, if it was one and not a misunderstanding, was wrong.
I remember explaining years ago that I build my systems
WITHOUT_KERBEROS to avoid interference from base Heimdal; perhaps you
read that to mean it could not exist. That was not what I meant.

> I would assume that the audience for the combination of FreeBSD
> and PostgreSQL using Kerberos is quite small. Can we perhaps limit

I would imagine that one of the reasons for this, if it is true, is that Kerberos/GSSAPI auth with PostgreSQL has been all but unusable for years, even considering only PostgreSQL <=15 where at least the build works.

> the amout of options?

It is the same set of options that just about every other port out
there that supports GSSAPI auth has as well. Look for example at www/nginx, ftp/curl, www/squid.

Counterexample is www/mod_auth_gssapi; IIRC the reason I hardcoded MIT there is that it just doesn't build with Heimdal. (I should probably take another stab at it.)

> Always have Kerberos on, with an optional knob to use one of the
> ports, is probably a good way forward. Does any of the suggested
> patches actually support that? For all varieties of Kerberos ports
> that exist?

Both existing patches, as far as I know, support the whole range of available Kerberos implementations, which is two and a half - security/heimdal, security/krb5-and-family, and base Heimdal if absolutely necessary.

You should prefer Cullum's patch over mine, but please consider my comment (16) in #220325.

As far as the latest two PostgreSQL versions are concerned, base Heimdal may as well not exist because they will not build with it, so the knob cannot be made all that optional there. The style of "one option to enable Kerberos, then another option group to override base with" is not how it is usually done, instead, as shown in Cullum's patch, there is an OPTIONS_SINGLE group with one entry for each applicable implementation including base, plus one for none-of-the-above.
